Description:
A nonprofit coffee shop tucked inside the Sparkhouse West community hub in west Charlotte's Ashley Park neighborhood, brewing locally roasted Enderly Coffee alongside espresso drinks and pastries. The 8,000 square foot space, renovated in the basement of Shiloh Institutional Baptist Church, doubles as the home base for Do Greater Charlotte's youth entrepreneurship programs and rents coworking desks and conference rooms to small business owners.

Expect a creative, community-driven atmosphere where teen baristas, remote workers, and local entrepreneurs share the room. Every cup directly funds programming for middle and high school students learning entrepreneurship, digital media, and graphic design. Originally opened in 2022 as Creative Lab Coffee, the shop relaunched under the Sparkhouse Coffee brand in February 2026.
Comments:
This place has some history. I visited back in 2023 when it was still called Creative Lab Coffee. I was leading a tech summer mentorship for high school students, and William McNeely invited our group out to learn about the intersection of art, music, and technology. The students did a hands-on activity where they got to DJ, use real DJ hardware and software, and then see how deeply technology is woven into the creative process. Hands down the best field trip we did that whole summer.

And there's another bit of history here. The viral 2023 TikTok of a kid DJ'ing and going "yes, of course". That came out of Backspinz Music Academy, an organization that partners with Do Greater Charlotte and meets in this exact space. Same building, same mission, just under the Sparkhouse name now.

So, when you're sipping coffee here, you're sitting in the room that helped to produce that viral moment, and you're funding the nonprofit that gave that kid the platform to discover what he could do. That's what makes this spot special. It's a lot more than just a coffee shop, they're out here changing lives! You can learn more about the Do Greater Foundation here.

Description: Independent coffee shop located inside PostScript in Charlotte’s Plaza Shamrock neighborhood. The space features a narrow, intimate layout with table seating, bar seating, warm wood finishes, dark walls, vintage mirrors, tile floors, and a moody café atmosphere. Offers coffee drinks in a restaurant setting with a quieter, tucked-away feel compared to larger open coffee shops. Free parking is available in the lot outside.
Comments:
I almost didn’t list this on the site because I don’t want y’all to go, tell all your friends, make a TikTok, and then suddenly it’s packed, sweaty, and impossible to find parking, even though they do have a free lot. But I committed to sharing info on good third places through this site, and I felt guilty not listing it because that wouldn’t be fair to the business. Just, please, be chill. Like you don't have to tell your entire friend group everything. Share this spot just with the 1-2 you really like, the S-tier friends.

Anyways, this is inside the restaurant PostScript, but it has its own side of the building. It's a long, moody hallway with table seating along one side and bar seating along the other. It's small, but they use every inch of the space well to provide seating. The room has a tucked-away feeling to it, with dark green walls, warm wood, old tile floors, globe lights, red curtains, a bunch of ornate mirrors, and a bar that makes it feel kind of like a European café hiding inside an old neighborhood restaurant. That's a very long way of saying it's not some cookie cutter aesthetic sort of place.

There’s some history in the space too. PostScript took over the building that was the restaurant Letty's which closed in 2025 after the owner retired. Dyad also has its own story, having spent the past few years as a pop-up inside other businesses before this became its dedicated location. So, the whole setup feels like a passing of the torch. A longtime neighborhood restaurant space becomes something new, and a coffee pop-up finally gets a home of its own.

The music they play here is really good. Soulful jazz, funk, R&B, sometimes some Americana. One of the few places I don't wear noise cancelling headphones so I can enjoy the ambience.

Description: Neighborhood coffee shop located in an apartment complex. Offers expertly brewed coffee, pastries, and a lofted workspace. The menu consists of traditional & trendy lattes, teas, hot chocolates, & plant-based energy drinks. People are our passion; great coffee is how we meet them!
Comments:
There's a story behind this spot. CHNO used to be in Plaza Midwood, before they had to suddenly close down due to beef with their landlord. They then became mobile, before opening in this location, which used to be a Night Swim, at the bottom of the Elan LoSo apartment complex. The location itself is wonderful. Small, but a hidden gem.

Aesthetically, it's posh, elegant, and modern. But say all those words in a French accent. A mix of crisp whites, natural greens with plants here and there, and gold accents on the light fixtures. Bravo to the interior designer of this place. The aesthetic is lovely.

The upstairs area is part of the coffee shop, and it's a gem. It's small, only 3 seating options, but they've adorned the overhang with plants, there's a restroom, it's carpeted, and 2 of the 3 seating options have outlet access. The coziest of cozy nooks. If you can get an upstairs spot, good for you. Downstairs is nice as well, a few two-person tables, couches, and coffee bar seating with outlets.

Parking is tight, it's around the corner in a garage, paid and 2-hour limited. But there's a few street spots that are free and not time limited. Overall, a lovely coffee shop. Very kind owner. Talk to her about coffee or teas, she's very knowledgeable.

Description: Embedded in the Twenty Two West studio and storefront is our cozy Chai Window, where we serve up cups of beautifully spiced, rich organic tea made in house from whole spices, velvety oat milk, and softly sweetened with coconut sugar. Each sip adds a layer of warmth, creating a true sip and shop experience!
Comments:
If there were an award for best smelling third place in Charlotte, this place would win. The chai smells marvelous, but also the locally made candles, incense sticks, and various other herbal, earthy, homey items that are sold in the same building by the store Twenty Two West.

The space is in a converted home which is mostly for Twenty Two West but The Chai Window has a couch, a table with three chairs, and some other tables here and there. So, it’s small. Super small, but charming, adorable even.

Don’t come here with your laptop to work or study, that doesn’t match the vibe. Just relax. Breathe in the pleasant smelling air. Peruse the gift shop. Sip chai. Kick back. Buy some incense. The Serenity Now blend with lavender, frankincense, and clove is wonderful.

Description: A bright, health-focused cafe in South End serving gourmet toast, smoothie bowls, cold-pressed juices, espresso drinks, wellness shots, and grab-and-go items. The space has a clean, polished feel with counter service, high-top seating, booths, and a quick daytime rhythm suited for breakfast, brunch, lunch, or a casual coffee meeting. The menu leans fresh and colorful, with options like avocado toast, sweet toast, acai bowls, smoothies, vegan-friendly items, and gluten-free bread. It works best as a light meal stop or casual daytime hang rather than a long, quiet work session.
Comments: If you moved to Charlotte from LA, you'll feel at home here. With both the pricing and the aesthetic. Valley girl accents are welcome, encouraged even, at this third place. They only need an Erewhon next door to complete the vibe.

Description: A large South End brewery and taproom set in a converted mechanic's shop, with 8,000 square feet indoors and nearly an acre of outdoor gathering space with fire pits, games, and plenty of room to spread out, according to the official location page. The space includes a main taproom with high ceilings, a secret parlor for pool, a cozy lounge, a loft, 40 taps, a full cocktail program, and wood-fired Neapolitan-style pizza. It is better suited for groups, casual dates, events, and lively social hangs than quiet laptop work, especially with recurring specials, trivia, music bingo, and live music.
Comments: They're known for their pizza. At least, that's what I was told the first time I visited. It was alright.

Description:
A coffee shop inside Sparkhouse Uptown, a three-level, 20,000 square foot entrepreneurship hub from the nonprofit Do Greater Charlotte, located on N Tryon Street in the heart of Uptown Charlotte. The building, owned by Harvey B. Gantt (Charlotte's first Black mayor), previously housed his architecture firm Gantt Huberman Architects.

Alongside the coffee bar, the space offers coworking, classrooms, screen-printing, and podcasting and production studios for early-stage entrepreneurs and creatives. Beyond Amazing Donuts, a local Black-owned doughnut maker, sells doughnuts (and cinnamon rolls) inside on weekdays. Like its sister location in west Charlotte, every cup of coffee funds Do Greater Charlotte's youth entrepreneurship programs.
Comments:
This is the Uptown sister to Sparkhouse Coffee | West, opening on Juneteenth (June 19, 2026) at 500 N Tryon. The setup is a three-level, 20,000 sq ft creative hub from Do Greater Charlotte inside a building owned by Harvey B. Gantt, Charlotte's first Black mayor (his old architecture firm used to be there).

Coffee bar, coworking, classrooms, podcast and production studios, screenprinting, the works. Per QCity Metro, the goal is "everything you need to grow a business should be in one building, from entrepreneurial support organizations to creative studios."

What I'm most hyped about is that the black-owned business Beyond Amazing Donuts will be selling inside on weekdays, and their cinnamon rolls are absolute🔥🙌🏾. That alone is worth the trip. And like the West location, every dollar funds Do Greater Charlotte's youth entrepreneurship programs.

Description: Locally owned neighborhood eatery, bar, and grab-and-go market located on The Plaza in Plaza Midwood. The space serves lunch, dinner, and weekend brunch, with a menu focused on pizza, sandwiches, salads, entrees, and Mediterranean-inspired flavors. It also offers beer, wine, cocktails, prepared meals, frozen take-home options, and gourmet market goods. Good for casual meals, patio dining, takeout, drinks, quick lunch, and relaxed neighborhood hangouts.
Comments:
Lively atmosphere. Bright, lots of natural light, and a well-sized patio area with a grassy patch where there are sometimes soccer goals for kids. As a result, this place is popular with families. I’ve seen a whole troop posted up here, with the parents drinking at the bar, the small kids playing outside on the patio, and the teenagers in a booth with their laptops studying.

If you’ve ever been to Rhino Market, it has a similar kind of neighborhood market feel, but with a much more expansive Mediterranean kitchen. The food is good. The lamb shank sandwich in particular is very much worth your attention. There’s plenty of seating, free parking out front, and more parking around the corner on Mecklenburg Avenue if the main lot fills up.

They also serve coffee and pastries, and there’s a convenience store section with cold drinks, snacks, pantry items, and frozen meals to go. So, it truly is a market. I like it. In the evenings, it gets packed out with families and the after-work drinking crowd at the bar, but during the daytime it’s a solid place to work remotely or catch lunch with a friend.

Description:
A woman-owned coffee shop and cocktail bar in Plaza Midwood designed as an all-day third place for morning coffee, co-working, and drinks later in the day. The former auto shop has a good number of indoor seats, industrial bones, warm feminine design details, and a dog-friendly patio.

The beverage program spans coffee, cocktails, mocktails, dirty sodas, THC drinks, beer, and wine, with house-made syrups like ube coconut, mountain berry, Thai tea, lavender vanilla, matcha banana pudding, brown sugar cardamom, chai, and espresso martini. Food is intentionally light, with nuts and pretzels instead of a full kitchen, plus rotating local vendor pop-ups planned
Comments:
I love that the owner is intentionally calling this a “third place.” It’s clearly built for lingering, with comfortable sofas, tables, and bar seating instead of a quick in-and-out coffee counter. And the staff goes out of their way to be kind, personable, and engaging. If you've ever seen those memes about cold, distant, overworked baristas that make you feel guilty for making an order, the staff here is the opposite. They're very friendly.

The building is a renovated auto shop, but it’s far from the very overdone industrial chic aesthetic. There’s nothing cookie cutter about the space. I’d describe it as unapologetically feminine. It feels deeply customized to the owner’s taste, with dark green walls, gold details, and a softer personality layered over the garage bones.

This would be a top tier spot for a women’s book club, or something of that nature. In the times I’ve visited, I’ve been the only man in the building, lol. It has a cozy, very feminine energy where you could imagine the cast of Gilmore Girls, Pretty Little Liars, or Gossip Girl sitting around with coffee, debriefing their lives, and somehow turning a casual conversation into an entire season arc.

And sometimes they have this vintage rotary phone setup gossip game thing. There are two phones inside where guests can record anonymous confessionals, and the team plans to turn those recordings into a social series. One pink phone says, “Spill the tea, tell us everything, sis,” which is the kind of playful, slightly dramatic touch that makes a place feel like more than just a cafe.

Also worth noting, Plot Twist sits in the same compound as Trope Bookshop and Wiloe Home & Gift, creating a small women-owned pocket just off Central Avenue. That makes the whole corner feel like a fun little Plaza Midwood hangout cluster, especially if you want coffee, books, gifts, and gossip all in one outing.

Description: A Middle Eastern roastery and coffee shop in Charlotte focused on fresh-roasted coffee, premium beans, and culturally rooted flavors. The concept pairs handcrafted coffee with sweets, nuts, and spices for a cafe experience that goes beyond a standard coffee stop. Visitors can expect a menu inspired by Middle Eastern hospitality, tradition, and craftsmanship. The space is positioned as a specialty destination for rich coffee flavors and imported-style treats rather than a typical grab-and-go cafe.
Comments: No idea when they're opening, but I drove past their location on April 26, 2026 while the windows were still covered for construction. This Instagram post indicates they're opening soon.

Description: A nonprofit cat cafe and animal rescue with a separate cafe area and cat lounge in downtown Monroe. Visitors can stop by the cafe for coffee, beer, wine, soda, pastries, local art, animal supplies, and merchandise, while cat lounge time is handled through reservations or walk-in availability (official booking page). The space is designed around adoption, with rescued cats living in a calmer lounge environment where visitors can meet them before applying to adopt
Comments:
You don't need a reservation to stop by the café and grab a beverage or pastry. The café is separate from the cat lounge, which does require a reservation, although they do allow walk ins, more details here.

I'd say this place is more of a "sip coffee and hang out with cats" vibe than a "camp out on your laptop" one. Stick with the theme, enjoy the presence of feline friends. The cafe is run by Charlie’s Angels Animal Sanctuary, whose stated mission is to let people meet animals in the cafe so adoptions are a better lifetime match.

Description: The Juice Pod in South End is a vibrant wellness cafe offering cold-pressed juices, superfood smoothies, and acai bowls. Located on the ground floor of The Line building along the Rail Trail, it serves as a modern, fast-casual destination for health-conscious locals and trail users.
Comments: Plenty of indoor seating and large windows that let in a ton of natural light. Rather pricy though.

Description: A family-run bakery and coffee shop in Kannapolis with a warm, pink-accented interior. The menu spans pastries like pistachio croissants, guava pastries, chocolate chip croissants, and chocolate macarons alongside coffee drinks including frappes and tiramisu iced coffee. Savory options like empanadas are also available. Staff are known for their friendly, personal service, and pastries can be warmed up on request. The space is small and clean, with a neighborhood feel that draws repeat visitors.
Comments:
If pink is your color, or if you have a sweet tooth, you'll love it here. It's owned by a Latina woman who fully embraces a feminine aesthetic throughout the space, and there's artwork and paintings on the walls from local Latina artists.

Small and cozy with super friendly staff. More of a place to meet a friend, chill, relax, then work or study. The sweet treat options are endless here. They do coffee too, but this is a bakery at heart, and the Latin pastries are the main thing. There are a lot of them. There's also ice cream and empanadas.

Place: The Giddy Goat Coffee Roasters | Dowd YMCA
Operating Hours: ["Monday: 6:30 AM - 7 PM", "Tuesday: 6:30 AM - 7 PM", "Wednesday: 6:30 AM - 7 PM", "Thursday: 6:30 AM - 7 PM", "Friday: 6:30 AM - 1 PM", "Saturday: 8 AM - 1 PM", "Sunday: 8 AM - 1 PM"]
Description: A lively coffee shop tucked inside the Dowd YMCA that feels far more social than you’d expect from a café in a gym. People genuinely hang out here, whether they’re working, catching up, or settling in for a while, and the steady flow of regulars gives it an easy neighborhood feel. It’s open to the public, so you don’t need a YMCA membership just to stop in. Parking is managed by Metropolis, and Giddy Goat gives you 3 free hours after a purchase when you scan the QR code.
Comments:
You don’t have to be a YMCA member to hang out here. The coffee shop is in the lobby, before the YMCA check-in desk. Parking is free for 3 hours and validated by a QR code by the checkout counter. When you park, you’ll need to register with Metropolis by scanning the QR code in the parking lot. After that, scanning the QR code inside will apply the 3-hour pass to your session.

It’s a very modern coffee shop with great seating options. It reminds me of a college library. Not in the traditional sense, in the modern "made for studying" way, like the ones at rich state schools. As a result, this is a top-tier place to work remotely.

The Dowd YMCA in general is a very social place, with seemingly every attractive person in Charlotte having a membership here. So, if you’re looking to mingle, this is a good spot to hang out. If you're not a member, you're limited to the lobby, but it's a thing for YMCA members to work out then hangout here afterwards.

There’s good seating, including a conference room and a larger table for groups, plus an outdoor patio with some comfy lounge chairs. And it's spread out, so it doesn't feel cramped hanging here. They even have those single-seat chairs with high walls that colleges usually have. I don’t know if I’m describing them right, but they’re basically little one-person booths with walls high enough that you can slouch and people won’t really be able to see your face. Cozy. I like it.

Aside from that, the coffee and tea are good, and so are the empanadas. The staff is also really nice. They’re always happy, like seeing you is the best part of their day. Golden retriever energy, which is great if you’re in need of a kind face on a tough day.

Operating Hours: ["Monday: 8 AM - 5 PM", "Tuesday: 8 AM - 5 PM", "Wednesday: 8 AM - 5 PM", "Thursday: 8 AM - 5 PM", "Friday: 8 AM - 6 PM", "Saturday: 8 AM - 6 PM", "Sunday: 8 AM - 6 PM"]
Description: An award-winning French patisserie and bistro cafe tucked inside Specialty Shops SouthPark, run by classically-trained French chef Sylvain Rivet. The menu spans traditional viennoiserie, house-baked baguettes and sourdough, elaborate pastries like eclairs, opera cakes, and Napoleons, and a bistro selection of sandwiches, quiche, and salads. Coffee comes from Forte Legato Roastery and teas are Dammann Freres from France, with wine, champagne, cocktails, and beer also available. The space has both indoor seating and an outdoor patio with heaters, making it a good spot for lingering over a croissant and a cafe au lait or sharing a bottle of wine with pastries.
Comments:
If you've been here before and thought "it's pretty small" like me, things have changed. They have an expanded dining area now with plenty of seating, and really good access to outlets. Free Wi-Fi, even a QR code menu if you're introverted and want to order on your phone.

They've catered this place to invite people to stay awhile, and it's great. It's very expensive, but the space itself is very nice. Clean, a bit posh without being pretentious, a good mix of two-person and four-person tables. The staff is nice and down to earth. I like it. They don't have cinnamon rolls technically speaking, but they having something called a Kouign-Amann which is basically a pretentious cinnamon roll. A rolled-up croissant thingy that any American would assume is a cinnamon roll at first glance.

Anyways, if you use the front entrance and only see outdoor seating and some tall bar style seats, know that you've got to walk around the corner to see the rest of the building. There's plenty of seating between that front area, the outdoor patio, and the inside dining room. It's a solid remote work spot as a result. It's big enough to bring a study group, some friends, or a date 😏, and feel confident you'll find a place to plop down (provided you don't visit at some known busy time, like Sunday afternoons).

A final note, for the first time ever since moving to Charlotte and visiting the many French bakeries here, I heard a conversation in actual French. Two very elegantly dressed ladies carried on in French for well over an hour during one of my visits here. I don't know if that means this place is more authentic than the rest, but it was cool to see/hear!

Operating Hours: ["Monday: 7 AM - 7 PM", "Tuesday: 7 AM - 7 PM", "Wednesday: 7 AM - 7 PM", "Thursday: 7 AM - 7 PM", "Friday: 7 AM - 7 PM", "Saturday: 7 AM - 7 PM", "Sunday: 7 AM - 7 PM"]
Description:
A health-forward cafe in the Carmel Commons Shopping Center offering clean acai bowls, organic coffee, ceremonial-grade matcha, gluten-free French crepes, protein shakes, and grab-and-go superfood bites. The space is the flagship concept from the teams behind Spoons and Raw Crunch and Coffee, two Charlotte-born brands that merged to create an all-day cafe built around whole, organic ingredients with no additives, gums, or artificial flavors.

Expect counter-service ordering, a wellness-driven menu with items like the signature Eden bowl (chia pudding and pitaya base with edible flowers and bee pollen), and locally roasted organic drip coffee. The vibe leans bright and intentional, designed for a mix of quick grab-and-go visits and slower sit-down meals with family or friends.
Comments:
So, the full brand name is "Eden - Made from Good" sometimes shortened to "Eden Good" but they generally just go by Eden. And yes, it's a reference to the Garden of Eden in the Bible. The about page of their website is very clear about the Christian reference, which I love!

There's good access to outlets, a good amount of seating, including some outdoors, and its bright, so it's a good remote work spot. The menu has a lot of really healthy sounding stuff I haven't tried, but if you're health conscious this seems a good spot. They're known for their crepes so definitely get one of those if you visit. And they serve proper herbal teas, loose-leaf, steeped, no bag in your cup, which I deeply appreciate as a tea person. There's coffee as well.

Operating Hours: ["Monday: 8 AM - 6 PM", "Tuesday: 8 AM - 6 PM", "Wednesday: 8 AM - 6 PM", "Thursday: 8 AM - 6 PM", "Friday: 8 AM - 6 PM", "Saturday: 8 AM - 6 PM", "Sunday: 8 AM - 3 PM"]
Description: Upscale French-inspired bakery and cafe in SouthPark with pastries, breads, sandwiches, coffee, and a polished all-day atmosphere. It feels more refined than a typical neighborhood coffee shop, making it a good pick for a casual breakfast, lunch, or meeting over pastries. Expect a stylish interior, strong baked goods, and a menu that leans classic and well-executed.
Comments:
The prices here reflect the bougie nature of SouthPark on the whole. If you’re on a budget, or have any hopes of one day owning a home, I’d suggest you limit your visits here to every once in a while. They have another location in Ballantyne called Brasserie Copain that’s a full on restaurant plus bakery, while this location is more bakery and cafe.

That said, it’s a sort of faux rustic French bakery in a posh neighborhood. Unfinished floor, bread everywhere you look, French music playing in the background. The bakery is inside a larger building with other businesses, it’s near the back past a restaurant. You can sit inside the bakery’s area, which has around 7 two person seats, or in the larger building which has a bunch of rather large tables. If you’re looking for a place for a group, this spot is great for that. The building has plenty of seating.

They have cinnamon rolls, which I appreciate, but they’re just alright. Not great, not terrible. I give them a pass though, cinnamon rolls are not a traditional French pastry. The problem this place, and most French bakeries seem to have, when making cinnamon rolls is overdoing the fluffiness of the cream cheese frosting, to the detriment of the roll overall.

We’re talking a cream cheese frosting that sort of sits like a dollop on top of the roll, and even with warming, doesn’t properly seep into the folds of the bread. It could also stand to be sweeter. So again, not great, but not terrible. I’m something of a cinnamon roll connoisseur, so forgive me if that sounded overly critical. Try it for yourself. Your mileage may vary.

Operating Hours: ["Monday: 5 AM - 8 PM", "Tuesday: 5 AM - 8 PM", "Wednesday: 5 AM - 8 PM", "Thursday: 5 AM - 8 PM", "Friday: 5 AM - 8 PM", "Saturday: 6 AM - 8 PM", "Sunday: 6 AM - 8 PM"]
Description: A walk-in-only Dunkin' location tucked into a small strip center on Zion Ave in Cornelius. Unlike many suburban Dunkin' locations, this one has no drive-thru, so all customers order inside. The interior features armchairs near the front and tables toward the back, offering a slightly more comfortable setup than a typical Dunkin'. Serves the standard menu of coffee, espresso drinks, donuts, bagels, and breakfast sandwiches. The location tends to be quieter and less hectic than nearby chain spots, making it a reasonable option for a quick sit-down coffee break.
Comments:
A community submission by Antoine. It's a Dunkin, no need to explain their offerings, you know what it is. Although I only just learned they no longer go by Dunkin Donuts, just Dunkin.

Anyways, most people are familiar with their locations, but this one's a bit different. It doesn't have a drive-thru, meaning everyone must come inside, and they've set things up to accommodate that. Described as "Small but rarely busy. Has good tables nestled in the back, and nice armchairs in the front." in the submission. No cinnamon rolls (a coffee roll is not a cinnamon roll) but plenty of donuts. A solid low-key option if you're in the area.

Description:
A TikTok-famous specialty coffee shop and cafe opening soon in South End. Known for an all-white interior accented with pops of yellow, their locations are designed to feel warm and intentional. The menu features signature iced lattes like the La La Latte with vanilla and "la la cloud," a Banana Cloud Latte made with fresh bananas, and a Matcha Squared with double ceremonial matcha. Avocado toasts and a truffle burrata toast round out the food side.

The Dallas-based chain operates 25+ locations across Texas, California, Arizona, and Tennessee, and this will be its first in North Carolina. La La Land is tied to the La La Land Foundation, a nonprofit that supports foster youth through mentorship and paid employment.
Comments:
La La Land Kind Cafe is taking over Bitty & Beau's Charlotte location, which closed on March 22nd, 2026. For those who knew that spot, that means you already know the parking situation. Paid street parking right in front, but if you're willing to walk, a free lot and garage at nearby at Atherton Mill. 

The space is a medium-sized with a covered outdoor patio. If they keep that patio, this will be a solid hangout spot. Two purpose-driven coffee shops in the same space back-to-back, interestingly enough. Bitty & Beau's hired people with disabilities, and La La Land hires foster youth aging out of the care system.

This is La La Land's first location on the East Coast outside of Nashville. They are enormous on TikTok (6+ million followers) thanks to their "Drive-By Kindness" series where they hand out free drinks to strangers. The whole brand leans into yellow as a color and a vibe. Their about page says it best: "Yellow means warmth. Yellow means kindness."

No opening date announced yet. The Charlotte Business Journal broke the lease news on April 1, and Hoodline picked it up the same week. Should be a spot worth checking out once its open!

Operating Hours: ["Monday: 7:30 AM - 7 PM", "Tuesday: 7:30 AM - 7 PM", "Wednesday: 7:30 AM - 7 PM", "Thursday: 7:30 AM - 7 PM", "Friday: 7:30 AM - 7 PM", "Saturday: 7:30 AM - 7 PM", "Sunday: 7:30 AM - 7 PM"]
Description:
A Colombian-rooted cafe on Central Avenue in East Charlotte, operating as a direct extension of the neighboring Manolo's Bakery. The menu features espresso and coffee drinks made with Colombian Arabica beans, a house-made gelato line, and a pastry case focused on high-end pastries and croissants rather than grab-and-go baked goods.

Housed in a converted hair salon suite, the space has a more intentional sit-and-stay feel than the original bakery next door. Named after an award-winning documentary about the bakery's founder, Manuel "Manolo" Betancur, a Colombian immigrant, social activist, and 2021 World Bread Hero.
Comments:
This is one of those places where when people say, "Charlotte has no culture," I want to say, sit down with Manolo and the rest of the businesses in this corridor of Central Avenue. Immigrants, people who've been in Charlotte since the '80s and '90s, who've built their lives here. You tell them this city has no culture because you moved to South End and only go to restaurants and coffee shops that influencers peddle to you on IG and TikTok and think that's all the city has to offer.

Manolo Betancur is legitimately one of Charlotte's most beloved food figures. His bakery (Manolo's) is consistently mentioned around town, particularly when it comes to best Latin pastries in Charlotte. The Changebaker Place is right next door to the original bakery, which used to be a hair salon. Lady Betancur (Manolo's wife) designed the space and runs it. The whole idea is that it's the elevated cafe experience complementing the bakery, with proper espresso drinks, gelato, cinnamon rolls 🙌🏾, various other pastries, and loose-leaf teas. So, you got cozy seating, laptop-friendly tables, fast Wi-Fi, meeting rooms, and top tier vibes.

The documentary connection is cool. "The Change Baker" premiered at Mountainfilm in Telluride and won an award before getting its Charlotte premiere at the Carolina Theatre, on the same day the cafe opened (January 17, 2026). The film follows Manolo's journey from Colombian Army captain fleeing war to Charlotte baker to activist. The Carolina Theatre listing describes the bakery as "a hub for activism, compassion and opportunity" with the motto "Made in America by the Hands of Immigrants."

I can confirm that's all true. I saw the film at The Changebaker Place as part of my participation in the 2025-2026 cohort of the 412 Fellowship (awesome organization and program, if you're a Christian in Charlotte looking to grow deeper in your faith and how it connects to your work, I highly recommend it!). I got to hang out with Manolo and learn more about him, the film, the parts they left out, the history of Charlotte, and his growing role as an activist. As a transplant, I can tell you that speaking with him was enough to make me never again say "Charlotte has no culture". It's simply not true!

Also, the gelato here has its own Instagram at @artisengelato, and Manolo runs a nonprofit called By Immigrant Hands out of the same address. The nonprofit coffee they sell supports bakers in Ukraine, which Manolo has visited three times to deliver aid. The depth of mission behind this place is rare!

Last note if you read this far. Check out Higher Grounds, a coffee shop by the same guy in Myers Park United Methodist Church. Top tier spot to hang out and get some work done.

Description:
A Vietnamese-fusion cafe near UNC Charlotte serving a wide range of Asian-inspired street food and drinks. The menu features pho made from bone marrow broth simmered for 30 hours, banh mi sandwiches, loaded fries, Korean corn dogs, and mochi donuts alongside boba milk tea, matcha drinks, fruit teas, and Vietnamese desserts (che).

Ordering is counter-service style where customers place their order and bring it to their table. This is the second location from owner Crystal Nguyen, expanding from the original spot in Steele Creek, and offers extended hours that make it a solid option for late-evening studying or hanging out near campus.

A neighborhood coffee shop opening soon in the Madison Park area on South Boulevard, this is a new location from chef and owner Craig Barbour's ROOTS Food Co. hospitality group. Dubbed "Roots Cafe 2.0," the space at Madison Corners is planned as a two-phase project.

The first phase will be a roughly 1,385-square-foot coffee shop with about 30 seats featuring Enderly Coffee roasts, pastries, a limited hot menu, and grab-and-go items like parfaits, fruit cups, and salads.

Phase 2 will add a full kitchen and expanded seating in an adjoining 1,127-square-foot space, enabling an expanded American menu focused on locally sourced ingredients from Freshlist, including breakfast burritos, roasted carrot bowls, and wraps.

Description: A coffee shop and bakery counter inside Gibson Mill Market, a locally sourced food hall housed in a historic 1899 textile mill in Concord. The menu centers on espresso drinks and lattes alongside matcha and tea options, paired with the bakery's signature hand-piped buttercream flower cupcakes and rotating seasonal treats. The space sits within the larger Gibson Mill complex, which also includes craft breweries, an antique mall, restaurants, and event venues. Visitors can order ahead online for pickup or stay to enjoy their drinks in the food hall seating area. The vibe is casual and community-oriented, fitting for a quick coffee stop or a longer visit spent exploring the rest of the market.
Comments: This spot is inside Gibson Mill Market, so the coffee shop itself is small, but the building it sits in is huge with plenty of space to spread out and stay awhile.

Description: Bubble tea shop serving a broad menu of milk teas, fresh fruit teas, craft sodas, refreshers, slushes, and matcha-based drinks. The menu goes beyond the basics with options like Hong Kong milk tea, rose oolong milk tea, fruit-and-Yakult blends, brown sugar swirls, and sparkling fruit sodas. Drinks are built around premium loose-leaf tea, fresh fruit, and dairy, giving the menu a more crafted feel than a standard boba stop. A solid pick for a sweet drink, casual meetup, or quick dessert run.

Place: Jilly Jelly Boba Tea and Snow
Address: 10915 Monroe Rd Ste D, Matthews, NC 28105
Operating Hours: ["Monday: 12:30 PM - 5:30 PM", "Tuesday: 12 PM - 6:30 PM", "Wednesday: 11 AM - 7 PM", "Thursday: 11:30 AM - 7 PM", "Friday: 11:30 AM - 7 PM", "Saturday: 11:30 AM - 7:30 PM", "Sunday: 12 PM - 6 PM"]
Description: Independent bubble tea and dessert spot serving a broad menu of shaken iced teas, milk teas, fruit drinks, slushes, and specialty flavors like Hokkaido, taro, honeydew, and dragon passion fruit. It goes beyond drinks with snacks and light bites such as onigiri, chicken teriyaki, and bubble waffles, giving it more range than a standard boba counter. Customer feedback consistently highlights fresh boba pearls, flavorful drinks, and warm, personable service. A good fit for a casual sweet treat, quick snack stop, or low-key hangout in the Matthews area.
Comments:
I think the overlap between people who like K-pop and people who would enjoy this place is probably pretty big. It’s cute and small, with maybe five tables max, but they use every corner of the building well. I’ve seen pairs of students hanging out here to study, which feels right for the space. The owner is very kind, and it’s obvious she’s put a lot of thought into the menu. If you have questions about the drinks, she’s more than happy to explain how everything works, and she’s generally very friendly and open to chatting.

On my first visit here, a family from Jamaica came in and casually mentioned jerk chicken to each other, which somehow opened the door to a whole conversation. The owner started sharing her own experiences trying Caribbean food spots in Harlem and Brooklyn, and from there it just naturally turned into a broader conversation about food in general. It was delightful to watch, and it felt like such a genuine third place moment. Lovely little shop.

Description: Casual bubble tea café serving a broad menu of milk teas, fruit teas, slushies, yogurt drinks, smoothies, and customizable toppings like boba, jelly, and popping pearls. It also stands out for offering Vietnamese bánh mì and snack items, giving it more of a light café feel than a drinks-only shop. The tea program emphasizes freshly brewed loose-leaf tea, and the overall positioning is a friendly hangout spot for families, friends, and students. Good for a quick drink run, an easy snack stop, or a relaxed low-key meetup in the Matthews area.
Comments: A bit of seating inside but more outside on their patio, so a solid spot on a warm day. Alongside bubble tea they have food, coffee, and hot teas. Free Wi-Fi but limited outlets so come charged up. The chai tea is known to be really good here, as well as the chicken bahn-mi. The whole place has a very family-owned local feel to it. I like it!

Description: A small, independently owned bubble tea franchise location serving boba teas, fruit teas, and craft sodas as other Milk Cha-Cha locations. The interior is clean, compact, and kawaii-decorated, with a few tables and photo-op spots inside. It is notably smaller than the Charlotte locations but offers the same drink quality with customizable toppings and one large cup size. Staff are frequently praised for being quick and friendly, and the shop plays K-pop music to set the vibe. It is one of the only dedicated boba tea shops in Rock Hill.

Address: 4620 Piedmont Row Dr Suite 170a, Charlotte, NC 28210

Description:
A locally owned bubble tea shop inside the Piedmont Town Center shopping plaza, specializing in boba tea, fruit teas, and craft sodas made with fresh fruits, fresh dairy, and premium loose leaf teas. The interior is bright, cozy, and kawaii-themed with boba Squishmallows, a wall of board games, armchairs, and Hello Kitty merch for sale alongside Asian snacks.

Drinks come in one size and are highly customizable with toppings like fruit jellies, popping boba, and classic tapioca. Popular orders include the Brown Sugar Milk Swirl, Fresh Strawberry Tea, and the Mango Matcha Latte, and the staff (called "bobaristas") are generally helpful with recommendations for first-time visitors.
Comments: It smells really good here. Good amount for seating for a small shop, but precious few outlets, so if you’re coming to work/study, charge your laptop.

Description:
A pop culture memorabilia shop on Main Street in downtown Mooresville that doubles as a mini museum and hangout spot. The store stocks action figures, Hollywood and music autographs, vintage sports cards, collectible statues, costumes, props, oddities like taxidermy and vintage medical devices, and an exclusive horror section in the back.

A lounge area and back-room arcade with a Wii and free-to-play games give visitors a reason to stay and hang out rather than just browse and leave. The owner is known for being approachable and encouraging visitors to take their time, with the store billing itself as a place where fans can "simply kick back and soak in a treasure trove of collectibles 25 years in the making."
Comments:
A community submission by Brad Kalmikoff (the owner), this place is truly unique! Brad turned a lifelong collecting obsession (starting with first edition Power Rangers figures in the '80s) into a full-blown community hangout in downtown Mooresville. The store has a lounge area, a back-room arcade with a Wii you can play for free with a purchase, a dedicated horror section, a life-sized Raptor, and monthly free Chicago-style hot dogs on every third Saturday.

He hosted RJ Mitte from Breaking Bad for a signing back in 2023, and his seasonal "THC RAVE" vendor events pull in 50+ vendors, food trucks, and a live band with no fees for anyone to set up. Brad describes the vibe as "welcoming all, especially those not easily offended or with super professional high expectations". Worth the drive to Mooresville if you're into pop culture, Pokemon, sports memorabilia, or just want to eat a free hot dog next to a dinosaur.

Description:
A specialty coffee shop founded by three friends, offering light roast and specialty-grade coffee, housemade food and baked goods, and scratch-made syrups. The interior features a calming, modern palette with indoor plants for sale woven throughout the space alongside lifestyle goods, creating an atmosphere that blends a plant shop with a cafe.

Visitors can expect pour overs, cortados, espresso drinks, and matcha, all prepared with careful attention to quality. The vibe is cozy, quiet, and well-curated, making it a solid spot for reading, light work, or a slow morning stop along the Cherry Rd corridor in Rock Hill.
Comments:
If you're into plants, you'll love this place. They're everywhere, all of them are for sale, and I love it. The whole shop smells wonderful as a result, and with plenty of tall windows, it's this combination of good smelling greenery, lots of natural light, and a very chill herbal sort of vibe. It's a small coffee shop but they use every inch of the building well.

There's a good amount of seating, a little gift shop area with trinkets and plants and whatnots, and a leather couch that's solid for lounging with a group of friends. I don't actually drink coffee so I'm no good for providing a review on that, but they have herbal teas that are great, steeped properly, no bag in your cup 👏🏾. They also have pastries, no cinnamon rolls, but the maple crusted pumpkin spice loaf is very good. Oh, and food too, so you can hang out here and get everything you need. Coffee, tea, pastries, food, cold drinks, all of it.

I think this is a solid spot to work from remotely. The natural light keeps things bright and energetic and being surrounded by plants is just nice. The name Flora comes from the Latin word for flower and the Roman goddess of spring and flowering plants, so it's no wonder they leaned all the way into the greenery.

The three women who founded this place (Colleen, Alyssa, and Jillian) started out as a mobile cart at the Charlotte Regional Farmers Market before opening this shop. All their syrups are made from scratch in-house. Parking is free. The lot is small, but you can park on the street for free nearby.

Description:
A cat cafe in Rock Hill combining a full coffee bar, a cat lounge with adoptable cats, a boutique, and cat boarding services. The cafe side serves specialty espresso drinks, frappes, matcha, chai, lemonades, and refreshers using beans from local roaster Forte Legato, paired with gluten-free pastries from Wild Flour Sweets and freeze-dried candies from The Magic Box.

The cat lounge is a separate, calm space where visitors can interact with friendly cats available for adoption through partnerships with local rescues including the Lancaster SPCA. Walk-ins are welcome when capacity allows, but booking ahead is recommended, especially on weekends. The space also includes a boutique featuring products from local makers.
Comments:
This is Rock Hill's first and only cat cafe, and it's a really well-thought-out operation. Inga Carey (the owner) went all in on the research, she literally visited 40+ cat cafes across the country before building this one. The cafe partners with Forte Legato for the coffee (local roaster) and the Lancaster SPCA for the cats, so you're supporting rescue efforts just by showing up.

For the cat lounge, no reservations are required, but there's a cost and they fill up on weekends and holidays, so booking ahead is smart. The building can be tricky to find, it's behind the Golden Corral on Cherry Rd. Look for the purple signs and enter via the Midas Auto Shop side off Cherry Rd, or from Anderson Rd across from the Clarion Hotel. They also run private parties in the cat lounge if you're looking for a unique event space.

One thing to note, this is more of a "hang out with cats and sip coffee" kind of place than a "camp out with your laptop for three hours" place. The cat lounge sessions are timed, but the cafe side is a more relaxed, open-ended hangout.

Description: A French-inspired bakery and cafe from New York with its first North Carolina location at The Commonwealth in Plaza Midwood. Known for its South of France aesthetic with vintage decor, whimsical floral accents, and warm lighting, Maman serves an all-day menu of breakfast items, sandwiches, tartines, salads, pastries, and specialty coffee.
Comments: Nationwide chain coming soon to Charlotte, evidence here. They got famous in 2017 when their nutty-chocolate chip cookie showed up on Oprah's favorite things list.

Description: Craft brewery taproom in South End pouring a rotating lineup of beers on draft, with additional bar options like cocktails. The space is positioned as dog- and family-friendly, with a rooftop patio known for skyline views and a relaxed hangout vibe. Food is typically available on site through a resident food option, making it easy to settle in for a longer visit. Good for casual meetups, weekend hangs, and post-work stops.
Comments:
Free Wi-Fi, great upstairs patio with a view of Charlotte’s skyline. Industrial chic aesthetic. Friendly staff, and there’s a coffee shop within, Cool Idiot Coffee, making this a solid place to hangout. Access to outlets is solid downstairs by the taller tables.

Their parking lot is free but super small, in the vicinity there’s some free parallel parking, some paid of the same variety, and some paid lots. So you’ll just have to float around and find where you fit.

Operating Hours: ["Monday: 7:30 AM - 3 PM", "Tuesday: 7:30 AM - 3 PM", "Wednesday: 7:30 AM - 3 PM", "Thursday: 7:30 AM - 3 PM", "Friday: 7:30 AM - 3 PM", "Saturday: 8:30 AM - 3 PM", "Sunday: 8:30 AM - 3 PM"]
Description: Compact coffee counter located inside HopFly Brewing in Charlotte’s South End, near the stairwell. The menu centers on espresso drinks alongside matcha, loose-leaf tea, hand-squeezed lemonade, and pastries. It works well as a quick caffeine stop, or as an add-on when you want a coffee while hanging out in the larger taproom it sits within.
Comments:
This spot is inside HopFly Brewing, which means there’s plenty of seating and space to spread out.  My favorite area is upstairs, there’s a side room area with some lounge chairs that face South Mint Street and then a patio with great views of the Charlotte skyline. Nice lowkey spot to hangout in an otherwise busy neighborhood.

The coffee shop itself has its own counter behind the breweries main counter downstairs. The menu is expansive, with house made syrups, all sorts of coffee options, some pastries (no cinnamon rolls but they’ve got some other nice options), herbal teas, and more. HopFly Brewing aesthetically is pretty standard Charlotte brewery, some variation of industrial chic, but I like it, they play good music. 

Free Wi-Fi so this is also a solid work/study option, just bring noise canceling headphones. Access to outlets is good downstairs by the taller tables. Parking is the biggest challenge here. There are a few free spots in a small parking lot, but it’s usually full, so it’s street parking from there that’s a mix of free parallel and some paid lots nearby.

Description: Artisan gelato shop serving Italian-style gelato made fresh on site with real ingredients like fruit and whole milk. Espresso and coffee drinks are also available, making it an easy stop for dessert or a quick café break. Expect rotating flavors and a simple, treat-focused menu built around gelato, coffee, and classic pairings like affogato. Great for a casual treat, quick date stop, or post-dinner dessert nearby.
Comments: Very nice spot in a walkable neighborhood. Coffee and ice cream, or gelato which is technically a different thing. It's small but they fill the place well with seating, so you can hang out if you'd like. Free Wi-Fi too.

Address: 618 W Franklin Blvd Suite D, Gastonia, NC 28052
Operating Hours: ["Monday: 7 AM - 9 PM", "Tuesday: 7 AM - 9 PM", "Wednesday: 7 AM - 9 PM", "Thursday: 7 AM - 9 PM", "Friday: 7 AM - 9 PM", "Saturday: 7 AM - 9 PM", "Sunday: 7 AM - 9 PM"]
Description:
A flagship "Exquisite" location from Waterbean Coffee, a local chain with 20 locations across the Charlotte region. The "Exquisite" concept is Waterbean's premium format, distinguished from their standard shops by the addition of a cocktail bar, live piano, Vietnam-inspired interior design, and an expanded food menu. The centerpiece is a wrap-around bar with espresso preparation on one side and cocktails on the other, all inside a 4,000+ square foot space in Gastonia's Fuse District.

The menu goes well beyond typical coffee shop fare, covering USDA-certified organic pour-over, siphon, Vietnamese coffee, espresso tasting flights, cocktails, loose leaf tea, and food items like the Dubai Chocolate Croissant and Ribeye Melt. Open daily until 9 PM, it serves as a comfortable spot for remote work, studying, or socializing.
Comments:
So, when I first moved to Charlotte, I had a few natives make comments to me about Gastonia that painted it like the elephant graveyard from The Lion King, a whole "you must never go there" sort of vibe. I'm happy to report that couldn't be further from the truth. Gastonia is fine, it has its own hidden gems, and this spot is certainly one of them. Shout out to Ty C. for putting me on to it!

This is Waterbean's first "Exquisite" location, which is basically their step-up concept. The difference from a regular Waterbean is that you get cocktails, live piano, and a design that pulls from their Vietnam location. And it does feel exquisite. It's a noticeably different vibe from walking into one of their standard shops.

To start, it's huge. Just the main area alone could seat well over 40 people but then there's a side room sort of spot that can fit about 20 more. Finding seating should never be a problem coming here, same with parking, plenty of space. They have all the menu items you've come to expect from a modern coffee shop, but also food (the Waterbean Special sandwich is great), and really good loose leaf herbal teas. The Candied Orange Green tea is great.

In terms of the aesthetic, it's polished. White faux marble tables, really comfortable black plush chairs, and a lot of accent lights all around to keep the place bright and energetic. It's an excellent spot to work remotely, great outlet access, just bring some noise cancelling headphones as they do play background music. If you really want to focus, go into the side room they have across from the checkout counter. It's open to all unless otherwise stated, sometimes it's booked for an event.

Address: 14835 Ballantyne Village Way Suite 130, Charlotte, NC 28277
Operating Hours: ["Monday: 10 AM - 10 PM", "Tuesday: 10 AM - 10 PM", "Wednesday: 10 AM - 10 PM", "Thursday: 10 AM - 10 PM", "Friday: 10 AM - 11 PM", "Saturday: 10 AM - 11 PM", "Sunday: 11 AM - 10 PM"]
Description: A dessert-focused ice cream shop in Ballantyne Village serving handcrafted gelato, artisanal chocolates, pastries, and espresso-based coffee drinks. The menu emphasizes variety, including dairy-free, vegan, and no-sugar-added options, with rotating featured flavors and occasional seasonal callouts. The space supports casual lingering with tables and tabletop games that make it practical for small groups or families. Hours run later than many dessert spots in the area, which makes it a useful evening option after dinner nearby.
Comments:
Credit to CATHZINE on Substack and Instagram for putting me on to this spot as a third place. It's more than just a grab and go ice cream spot, as they have free Wi-Fi, a good amount of comfortable seating, plenty of parking, outlets, and friendly staff.

They’re open pretty late most days, so you can camp out here in the evening with your laptop, or with friends, and you'll usually be one of the few, as it's more popular during the day. There's coffee, tea, pastries, but no food, so don't come hungry. Still, this is a great low key spot to hang out. The owners sweet and gentle French bulldog hangs around here a lot. There’s usually a ball hanging around you can use to play fetch, or you can just pet him/her (couldn’t tell)! 

I’d say this place is great for chilling and reading a book, or meeting friends, or just sitting down to eat ice cream and stare at the very nicely adorned walls. They have Wi-Fi so you could work from here, but the vibe feels more relax, stay awhile, and leave work behind. Last tip, there’s a parking garage for the complex on the back side, so head there if you can’t find parking out front.

Description: Authentic artisan French bakery and cafe in Cornelius serving a pastry case full of laminated favorites like croissants, pain suisse, and a cinnamon roll, plus macarons and classic patisserie. The menu goes beyond sweets with breakfast items, quiche, baguette sandwiches, and cheese and charcuterie boards, so it works for a quick pastry run or a slower sit-down lunch. Espresso and signature lattes round it out, and the overall vibe is a European bakery stop that feels more like a destination than a convenience pickup, especially if you arrive early before items sell out.
Comments:
Shout out to Alicia S. for finding this spot and being the source of all this good information! This place is so authentically French that some of the staff speak French, one of the owners is French, French conversation hours are hosted here by local language academies, and most people who've visited praise the food and pastries for being legit French (sorry Amelie's, I love you as a place to hang out but there are Frenchier French bakeries around town). 

Another thing to love about this place is the teas. They serve Harney & Sons, which is about as good as it gets when it comes to tea brands used by businesses. The place is co-owned by the couple Alexandre Darbousset and Angela Yeo. The two spent years planning the bakery after moving to the Lake Norman area and noticing there were no authentic French bakeries around. Alexandre brings traditional French baking techniques to the business, and they even source their flour from a French flour mill, Grands Moulins de Paris, to keep things legit.

Also, while it's not a traditional French pastry, they have cinnamon rolls, which I deeply appreciate 🙌🏾🙏🏽. They’re the best cinnamon rolls of any French bakery in the Charlotte area hands down. 10000% better than Amelie’s. Give them a try, at first glance, they look a bit dry, but looks can be misleading, and in this case they are. They’re great!

And speaking of authentic French bakeries in the Charlotte area, another one worth checking out is Mado Bakery and Cafe in Rock Hill. It's owned by a West African couple that lived in France for years before moving stateside. It's also great!

Address: 401 S Tryon St Ste 140, Charlotte, NC 28202
Operating Hours: ["Monday: 7 AM - 6 PM", "Tuesday: 7 AM - 6 PM", "Wednesday: 7 AM - 6 PM", "Thursday: 7 AM - 6 PM", "Friday: 7 AM - 6 PM", "Saturday: 7 AM - 6 PM", "Sunday: Closed"]
Description:
One of two Gotcha Matcha & Espresso locations in Uptown Charlotte, this one is inside Three Wells Fargo Center at Suite 140, right by The Green park. The entrance can be tricky to spot from the street since it is inside the office tower's ground-floor retail area rather than a traditional storefront. The shop itself has a few seats inside, and there is a good amount of seating in the hallway outside the storefront within Three Wells Fargo, so finding a place to sit is not usually a problem

Expect the same core menu of matcha lattes, espresso drinks, refresher teas, and pastries, plus some cool additions like Earl Grey matcha, lemon pound cake matcha, Vietnamese iced coffee, Ube cinnamon roll, and the Ube bagel with Ube cream cheese. The space has a brighter, more storefront feel with easier walk-up access compared to the original 200 S Tryon location, and it works well for a daytime coffee run, a casual meet-up near The Green, or a short laptop session. Their other location is about two blocks north at 200 South Tryon.
Comments:
This is their second location in Uptown. It's by The Green inside Three Wells Fargo center, as laid out in this Instagram post. They've got a full coffee shop menu in addition to loose leaf teas and plenty of sweet treats. It can be tricky to find, but look for the signs, and ask anyone around because they can usually point you to it.

Let me get to what's most important though. If there were an award for the most creative cinnamon roll in Charlotte, this spot would win. Magnificent. Extraordinary. Sublime. Transcendent. A revelation. Per this TikTok post, as well as their menu, and my own first-person account, they have what are called "Ube cinnamon rolls." I'm something of a cinnamon roll connoisseur, yet my visit here was my first time hearing of such a thing. So, I asked the staff, and did some research of my own, and discovered that Ube cinnamon rolls are "a Filipino-inspired twist on the classic American cinnamon roll, characterized by its vibrant purple color and a unique earthy, sweet flavor."

I can attest to their brilliance, specifically because of how different these are from a standard cinnamon roll. They are extremely generous with the purple Ube icing, and the roll itself has a sort of brown bread base, maybe even wheat, that comes across as a tad healthier than white bread, maybe to offset the extravagance of the Ube. It's also huge. 

So altogether, you get a massive cinnamon roll with colors that pop and flavors that pop, where the Ube icing is sweet but not the overwhelming buttercream sweet you may be used to in cinnamon rolls. It's just different, in a good way. I really think you should try it, then contact me to let me know what you think. I want to hear from you if the topic is cinnamon rolls.

What a splendid thing this is. For cultures around the world to bring their own unique flavors to the beauty that is a cinnamon roll. This is what we need y'all. Coming together worldwide over ways to bring your own flair to cinnamon rolls. Yes. Cinnamon roll diplomacy. I dig it.

Description: Cozy local coffee and boba spot in Denver with a customizable drink menu that covers espresso drinks, milk teas, fruit smoothies, and blended frappes. Often used for casual hangouts, quick work sessions, and small meetups, with a relaxed neighborhood feel and friendly baristas.

Description: Specialty coffee shop in Uptown on Church Street with a fun, design-forward vibe and a short seasonal drink menu alongside espresso basics. Seating is limited but cozy, and it works well for a quick laptop session or a meet-up if you do not need silence. Free Wi-Fi is available, and nearby garage parking is typically the move, with validation commonly offered for a limited window.

A luxury leaning Yemeni specialty coffee concept opening soon on East Morehead with a modern cafe vibe built around Yemeni inspired flavors and a pastry offering. This exciting addition to Charlotte's growing Middle Eastern coffee shop scene is tentatively scheduled to open in May 2026. See this Instagram post for details.

Operating Hours: ["Monday: 6:30 AM - 8 PM", "Tuesday: 6:30 AM - 8 PM", "Wednesday: 6:30 AM - 8 PM", "Thursday: 6:30 AM - 8 PM", "Friday: 6:30 AM - 8 PM", "Saturday: 6:30 AM - 8 PM", "Sunday: 6:30 AM - 8 PM"]
Description: Artisanal donut and coffee shop with chef-made, small-batch donuts and a full coffee menu in a bright, modern grab-a-box-or-hang-for-a-bit setup.
Comments:
Small spot, but they use the space well. There are 2 small two-person tables, 1 high-top table, a 6-person table in the middle, and 6 bar-style seats along a high table near the window. Still, I’d say it’s better suited for a quick chat than an extended stay.

That said, the pricing for donuts in bulk is wild. $52 for a dozen. I don’t even know how to process that kind of pricing. Inflation is bad, but is it $52 for a dozen donuts bad?

Parking can be tricky. It’s not obvious, but there’s a paid garage behind the building that belongs to an apartment complex. If you’re feeling bold, you can try the nearby CVS Pharmacy lot, though there’s always a risk of getting towed. Oh, and they have cinnamon rolls. They’re solid.

Address: 354 George W Liles Pkwy Suite 60, Concord, NC 28027

Description:
Family run tabletop and trading card game shop in Concord built around community play, with a cozy game space where cards clash, stories ignite, and adventures are always a roll away. Expect a curated mix of Magic: The Gathering, Star Wars Unlimited, Dungeons & Dragons, sports cards, and collectibles, plus open tables for casual play, Commander nights, and beginner friendly events.

There is a $5 day pass option that gives access to a growing board game library, and it is also free to browse, shop, or hang out at the counter with no pressure. Snacks and cold drinks are available daily including sweet and salty options, with THC drinks on hand and local beer and wine planned for the future for guests 21+. The vibe is welcoming for first timers and regulars, and staff are happy to help with demos, deck advice, and getting started.

A chai-and-specialty-coffee concept is opening soon in Charlotte with a brand story rooted in Pakistani chai culture, aiming to be a true “third place” for gathering beyond home and work. Expect karak chai as the headline, plus specialty coffee and a dessert-forward menu, with the Charlotte location reported for Uptown’s Fourth Ward near 5th & Poplar.
Haraz continues their expansion into the greater Charlotte area, this time in Indian Land, South Carolina, as evidenced by this Instagram account. Opening soon at CrossRidge in Indian Land.

As per this Instagram post, Haraz is opening a new location in Fort Mill. The Fort Mill location has been announced near the new CAVA and Earth Fare, so expect an easy pop-in spot for grab-and-go or lingering with a drink once it opens.

Description: Modern brewery and restaurant in Birkdale Village that doubles as a coffee bar in the mornings and a lively taproom and kitchen later in the day. Inside you will find high ceilings, a long bar, communal tables, and standard dining tables, with additional seating on a patio that faces the main village walkways. The menu runs from espresso drinks and breakfast items in the earlier hours to burgers, bowls, and shareable plates alongside house beers and cocktails at lunch and dinner. With free village Wi-Fi and plenty of seating, it can work for casual laptop time during slower parts of the day, though the space gets noisy and more social during peak meal and evening hours.

Address: 8320 University Executive Park Dr B, Charlotte, NC 28262
Operating Hours: ["Monday: 8 AM - 11 PM", "Tuesday: 8 AM - 11 PM", "Wednesday: 8 AM - 11 PM", "Thursday: 8 AM - 11 PM", "Friday: 8 AM - 1 AM", "Saturday: 8 AM - 1 AM", "Sunday: 8 AM - 11 PM"]
Description: Yemeni style coffee shop in University City with a big, modern interior, soft lighting, and lots of seating for singles, pairs, and small groups, plus some outdoor tables when the weather is good. Expect a long menu of espresso drinks, traditional Yemeni coffee and tea, and a pastry case loaded with saffron milk cakes, cheesecakes, and savory pastries rather than simple drip and muffins. The shop keeps long hours, draws a mix of students and remote workers during the day, and stays lively into the evening as a social hangout for people who want a non-alcoholic spot to meet up, study, or talk over dessert and coffee.
Comments:
Haraz is now undoubtedly a staple in the Charlotte area, with this being their fourth location after Plaza Midwood, LoSo, and Ballantyne (with even more locations on the way). And like all their other spots, it’s gorgeous. Polished faux marble tables, black and gold accents everywhere, every corner of the place pristine and clean. Gives it a very calming vibe, with this spot specifically having large floor to ceiling windows letting in a lot of natural light.

There’s plenty of seating, with my favorite spots being the two person tables back towards the far wall. Feels out of the way and snug. If you’ve been to the Plaza Midwood location and struggled to find seating, that’ll be less of a problem here. They even have a living room looking lounge area with a couch and glass doors that I believe you can book if you’re bringing a group. 

Parking right next to the store is in a garage, but the general area has a hotel and other businesses with lots you can park in if it’s full. Access to outlets is solid but not great, the booth style wrap around seating thing in the middle has no outlets, which is a bummer because those are great seats. But there are some along the back wall and near the entrance seating.

But let’s get to the most important thing here. This is the only Haraz location (that I know of) that has cinnamon rolls. They're medium sized, with cream cheese frosting that tastes almost exactly like the kind used by Amelie's, and pair nicely with a hot tea or chocolate. I’m glad I came here rather than assuming it’d be like all the other Haraz locations. I mean, it is in terms of decor, but that’s a good thing because all their locations are gorgeous. But having cinnamon rolls? This is now my favorite of all of them. I’m devoutly Christian, but they had me singing Alhamdulillah 🙌🏾🙏🏾 (which is the Arabic equivalent of Hallelujah and means praise be to God or thank God). Cinnamon rolls make any location better!

Description:
Small Christian coffee shop on South Tryon rooted in Biblical principles and focused on more than just serving coffee. The owners describe it as a kind of sacred space where coffee is a bridge for connection, conversation and hospitality that reflects Christ’s love. 

You will find handcrafted espresso drinks, frappes, teas and a rotating selection of baked goods, along with gospel focused open mics, mixers and other community events that blend culture, community and faith. The atmosphere is intentionally warm and ministry minded rather than corporate, with an emphasis on people leaving refreshed, encouraged and more grounded in their sense of purpose.
Comments:
The very definition of a hidden gem. I found out about it by word of mouth (shout out to Istar!). Owned by Eritrean locals who’ve filled the space with both Habesha culture and nods to their Christian values, which I love as a Christian myself. It’s one of those spots that feels genuine the moment you walk in.

There’s a bit of outdoor seating and more inside through the back room. When you first step in, you’ll see a couch and a few high-top tables, but head to the back and you’ll find more spots to sit, including a big table that’s great for groups. It’s compact but cozy.

Free Wi-Fi, great coffee, and a warm, friendly staff make it easy to stay awhile. They also host music nights, including gospel saxophone performances and other Christian-themed events that give the place real community energy. A true third place in every sense.

Address: 330 Camp Rd Suite B, Charlotte, NC 28206
Operating Hours: ["Monday: 12 PM - 7 PM", "Tuesday: 12 PM - 7 PM", "Wednesday: 12 PM - 7 PM", "Thursday: 12 PM - 7 PM", "Friday: 12 PM - 8 PM", "Saturday: 11 AM - 7 PM", "Sunday: 11 AM - 6 PM"]
Description: Used bookstore at Camp North End with bright floors, tall shelves of gently loved paperbacks and hardcovers, and a small selection of new titles from local authors. The space is connected to a coworking hub and set up as a gathering spot, with cozy seating, spots to read or work on a laptop, and coffee available. Regular author events, book clubs, and community gatherings make it an easy place to drop in, browse for a while, and then wander out into the rest of Camp North End’s food stalls, art, and outdoor seating.
Comments:
A friend of mine mentioned she attends a Silent Book Club here. I'd never heard of that concept, and now that I have, I love it. You meet up with a group of people, you all bring a book, you read in silence for a set amount of time, and after you've all read, you then socialize. It's a beautiful concept and now I want to try it. Our attention spans are lower than ever with digital devices pulling us away from reading all the time. Having a community to just sit and read with, that's just wonderful man.

This spot is in Camp North End, a third place in and of itself, which makes the whole area worth visiting. On warm days, the seating area right outside the shop is great. It's in a sort of enclave section that makes it feel snug. And back behind the building there's a great spot with those swing bench things that's perfect for reading in seclusion. When they host events, they expand into nearby Hygge Coworking, so while the shop is small, they end up with plenty of space to host large groups.

Operating Hours: ["Monday: 7 AM - 10 PM", "Tuesday: 7 AM - 10 PM", "Wednesday: 7 AM - 10 PM", "Thursday: 7 AM - 10 PM", "Friday: 7 AM - 11 PM", "Saturday: 7 AM - 11 PM", "Sunday: 7 AM - 9 PM"]
Description:
Casual spot at Camp North End featuring indoor and outdoor pickleball courts, golf simulators, a bar and Tex Mex food, all set up for groups to play or just hang out. There's a wide variety of seating from sofas to tables, with free Wi-Fi and plenty of free parking around the campus.

You order by scanning a QR code on your phone, so staff only check in every now and then, which makes it easy to settle in for a while without worrying about table turnover. The space is lively with steady background noise rather than quiet study vibes, and the food is cheap and tasty enough to carry you through a long session of games and people watching.
Comments:
Fun fact, I got to tour this spot before it opened while helping plan a pickleball tournament for a nonprofit I volunteer with, the Carolina Youth Coalition. They support high-achieving, under-resourced students on their journey to and through college. If you’re looking for ways to get involved, you should absolutely volunteer with them!

As for Tipsy Pickle, I got to see this place transform from an unfinished shell into one of the hottest pickleball and social spots in town. Our tournament went great, largely thanks to the Tipsy Pickle team who handled everything the day of, from food to drinks to logistics. We just focused on the prep work, and they made the event a success.

It’s located in Camp North End, which means free parking in the big gravel lot toward the back and plenty of other things to do nearby. Great location and an even better atmosphere. The food and drinks are solid, the vibe is lively, and it’s a great place not just to play pickleball but to grab a bite, sit at the bar, and hang out. There are two levels, plus plenty of outdoor space, so if you're looking for a place to meet a group of friends, this is a great pick.

Operating Hours: ["Monday: 7 AM - 10 PM", "Tuesday: 7 AM - 10 PM", "Wednesday: 7 AM - 10 PM", "Thursday: 7 AM - 10 PM", "Friday: 7 AM - 10 PM", "Saturday: 8 AM - 10 PM", "Sunday: 8 AM - 9 PM"]
Description: Informal, contemporary hangout with indoor and outdoor pickleball courts, craft cocktails and gourmet coffee.
Comments:
Plenty of space here. Definitely not the spot if you’re looking for peace and quiet, but top tier if you want a lively, energetic, and social crowd. You don’t have to come for pickleball either. It’s also a restaurant, bar, and café serving coffee, pastries, lattes, smoothies, and tea. Whatever you’re in the mood for, they’ve got it.

If you’re planning to hang out and work on your laptop, bring noise-canceling headphones (the Sony XM4s are still the all-time champs). There’s always music playing and the sound of pickleball paddles in the background, but it’s still a solid work spot if you don’t mind a little noise. Mornings are much calmer, and there’s a ton of lounge-style seating, comfy couches, and tables. If it’s warm, there’s also tons of outdoor seating.

The food is solid, parking is free, and the staff are really friendly. It’s clear they’re intentional about making people feel welcome and giving the space a laid-back, friendly vibe. Everyone I’ve talked to has been easygoing and helpful. Good vibes all around.

Place: To The Table Board Game Cafe

Description: Cozy board game cafe in Matthews offering a rental library of modern board games, trading card games, and role-playing games alongside drinks and light snacks. Guests pay a modest table or game fee, pick a title from the shelves, and settle in at cafe style seating or themed rooms for a few hours of play with friends or organized events like Commander and D&D nights. Located in a neighborhood shopping center with on site parking and free Wi-Fi, it caters to both serious gamers and casual groups looking for a laid back spot to play, meet new people, and try new games.

Description: Family friendly tabletop shop in Mint Hill with walls of board games, card games, and RPG supplies plus plenty of in store tables for open play. A large game library lets you try new titles in the space or rent them to take home, and there are private rooms and regular events for RPGs, trading card games, and miniatures. Located in a suburban shopping center with easy lot parking, it functions as a community hub where locals drop in to browse, learn new games, and spend hours playing with friends.

Description:
Downtown Concord game and hobby shop located in a historic former bank building, offering a wide selection of board games, trading card games, RPGs, puzzles, and miniatures. The main floor doubles as a retail space and play area, with multiple tables set up for casual gaming, tournaments, puzzle competitions, and regular events for games like Magic, Pokémon, and Warhammer.

The staff are known for being welcoming and knowledgeable, helping new and experienced players find the right games or cards. Snacks, soft drinks, and treats are available, free Wi-Fi makes it easy to look up rules or deck lists, and customers typically spend several hours here during leagues and events. Parking is via nearby public decks and downtown street parking.

Description:
Neighborhood comic and game shop in Northwest Charlotte that combines new comics, graphic novels, and trading card products with a family friendly play space. The current location has an expanded seating and group play area, plus a connected ice cream counter offering shakes, floats, and a big selection of cold drinks.

Regular events for Magic the Gathering, Pokémon, Dungeons & Dragons, and other games draw a consistent community of players of all ages. Private lot parking is available on site, and the vibe is more hang out and play games than sit quietly and work on a laptop.

Address: 1640 Sardis Rd N Ste 110, Charlotte, NC 28270

Description:
Board game cafe and wine bar in southeast Charlotte with a library of modern and classic tabletop games, a full coffee and tea program that includes a reservation only coffee omakase at the bar, and a menu of sandwiches, waffles, gelato, and small sweets.

Guests can reserve time or walk in to play from a curated list of family friendly and strategy games while enjoying espresso drinks, teas, beer, cider, and wine by the glass or bottle. The space is set up with a mix of lounge seating and game tables suited for couples, families, and small groups, and offers free Wi-Fi for light laptop use between turns.

Description: Outpost of a local mini-chain for organic produce, wine, craft beer & gourmet meats & cheeses.
Comments: Has some solid outdoor seating on the rail trail, great for people watching, or watching the light rail go by. They've gone viral a few times, at least within Charlotte, for their hot sandwiches. Small interior but there are tables here and there and free Wi-Fi (ask staff for the details, and a hint, it's a capital H and capital G in the password). I'd say this space is best suited for using their patio to meet friends and chill. If you need more space, it’s a short walk from Night Swim.

Operating Hours: ["Monday: 7 AM - 5 PM", "Tuesday: 7 AM - 5 PM", "Wednesday: 7 AM - 5 PM", "Thursday: 7 AM - 5 PM", "Friday: 7 AM - 6 PM", "Saturday: 8 AM - 6 PM", "Sunday: 8 AM - 6 PM"]
Description: Specialty coffee shop from a local Charlotte roaster serving carefully crafted espresso drinks, drip coffee, and rotating single origin offerings, along with a small selection of pastries. Known for consistent quality, attention to detail, and baristas who can help you choose something new, it is a reliable stop whether you are grabbing a quick coffee or exploring more adventurous seasonal drinks.
Comments: Super small, most of the seating is coffee bar style with high chairs, so maybe not a place if your goal is to stay a while.

Address: 1101 Central Ave Unit B, Charlotte, NC 28204

Description: Intimate vinyl listening bar tucked inside the lobby of a boutique hotel on Central Avenue, with a cozy lounge, projector visuals, and a high-quality sound system focused on curated DJ sets and deep cut playlists. Expect craft cocktails, wine, beer, and a few spirits free options, but no kitchen, so you'll need to bring or order in your own food.
Comments:
Found by a friend of mine, Alex Jones (not the controversial right-wing figure, this Alex is a black woman, just an unfortunate coincidence with the name, she's way cooler than the other Alex Jones). 

This is in a unique hotel called Hotel Refuge, worth checking out, they have an interesting story. The space itself for the bar is small with limited seating that suits dates or groups of four or fewer, and it can get busy on weekends, so reservations are recommended. I'd say it works best as an evening hangout spot for music lovers rather than a full-on work or study space.
